planned_launches: Y1: Wealth Management launch (130K users + onboarding)

client_concerns: Y1 upfront cost too high ($1.4M in original quote)
Budget approval cycle requires lower initial commitment
Need gradual cost ramp aligned to business value and launch timeline
sensitivities: Must maintain approximately $8M total over 5 years
Y1/Y2 costs must be significantly lower than $1.4M for board approval
Willing to accept higher Y3-Y5 costs if early years reduced
Costs should align with launch milestones (Wealth Y1, Retail/SME Y3)
constraints.notes: Client rejected original quote due to high Y1 cost. Need pricing structure with lower Y1/Y2 (ideally under $1M) that ramps to full value by Y3-Y5. Total must remain ~$8M. Consider milestone-based or graduated pricing.

deal_context.deal_classification: Large multi-segment new logo with phased rollout requiring milestone-based pricing
deal_context.baseline_summary: New logo with no existing commercial relationship. Client seeks a comprehensive multi-segment platform (Wealth, Retail, SME) with phased deployment starting with Wealth in Y1-Y2 (130K users) and full retail/SME launch in Y3 (scaling to 1.88M users).
client_pressure_points: Board approval requires Y1/Y2 spend under $1M (vs. $1.4M quoted) — budget cycle constraint is binding
Multi-year budget approval requires demonstrable value before full commitment — gradual ramp needed
14x user growth from Y2 to Y3 creates adoption risk — client wants cost protection during proof-of-value phase
Total budget ceiling of ~$8M over 5 years limits pricing flexibility
backbase_pressure_points: New logo in strategic APAC market (Sri Lanka) — win sets precedent for regional expansion
Multi-segment deal (Wealth + Retail + SME) creates high platform stickiness and cross-sell foundation
Managed hosting embedded in 2025 model protects recurring services revenue
500 Assist employees (likely wealth advisors) at premium rates ($500-$1200/employee) = $250K-$600K/year ARR opportunity
1.88M end-state users at $2-3 blended ARPU = $3.76M-$5.64M/year potential by Y3-Y5
strategic_considerations: Phased launch aligns perfectly with milestone ramp: Wealth (Y1-Y2) at reduced rates, full Retail/SME (Y3+) at full rates
2025 edition model may not fit cleanly — client has only 130K users Y1-Y2 but wants Retail Essential + SME Essential + Wealth Premium (multi-edition complexity)
Wealth segment justifies premium pricing ($15-50/user) but only 130K users Y1-Y2 — need to separate Wealth from Retail/SME pricing tracks
Assist Premium for 500 employees can anchor Y1 ARR at $250K-$600K (lower than $1M threshold but contributes to floor)
Total $8M over 5 years implies $1.6M average/year — current quote of $1.4M Y1 is reasonable but must be restructured to back-load
Risk: Milestone ramp may compress margin in Y1-Y2, but new logo strategic value + long-term ARR upside justifies concession
relevant_benchmarks: Wealth ARPU: $15-$50/user (130K users Y1-Y2 = $1.95M-$6.5M/year at full rate — too high for Y1 budget)
Retail ARPU: $1.50-$5.00/user (1.88M users Y3+ = $2.82M-$9.4M/year at full rate)
2025 Retail Essential base fee: $494K/year (covers 20K users) — client has 130K Y1, so additional 110K users at tiered rates
Multi-segment new logo ARR: Typical $500K-$3M, this deal targets $1.6M average ($8M/5 years)

recommended.label: Phased Value Ramp — Wealth-First Launch
recommended.description: Milestone-based pricing aligned to launch phases: discounted rates Y1-Y2 for Wealth pilot (130K users), full rates Y3+ for Retail/SME scale (1.88M users). Protects client budget in proof-of-value phase while capturing full ARR at maturity.
recommended.licensing_logic: Per-user pricing with segment differentiation. Y1-Y2: 60% of full rate for Wealth users (130K). Y3: 85% of full rate for all users (1.88M). Y4-Y5: 100% of full rate. Assist Premium charged at full rate all years (500 employees).
recommended.true_up_mechanism: Annual true-up based on peak concurrent users per quarter. True-up invoiced within 30 days of year-end.
recommended.cap_or_floor: Floor: $700K/year minimum ARR (protects Backbase). No cap — upside shared if users exceed projection.
pros_client: Y1-Y2 costs reduced to ~$700K-$750K (vs. $1.4M quoted) — fits board approval budget constraint
Cost ramp aligns perfectly with launch milestones: low during Wealth pilot, scales at Retail/SME launch
Total 5-year cost stays within ~$8M envelope while proving value before full commitment
pros_backbase: Strategic APAC new logo win with multi-segment platform stickiness (Wealth + Retail + SME)
Floor protects $700K/year minimum ARR — mitigates adoption delay risk
Y3-Y5 full rates capture $5M+ ARR at maturity (1.88M users at $3.20 blended + Assist + modules)
Services revenue front-loaded ($850K Y1) — positive cash flow profile
cons_client: Rising costs Y1→Y3 require internal communication (mitigate: tie to launch success metrics)
Annual true-up process adds compliance overhead vs. fixed block pricing
cons_backbase: Y1-Y2 margin compressed by 40% discount — delays ARR ramp vs. full-rate new logo
Client may push to extend ramp if Retail/SME launch delays beyond Y3

alternative.label: Segment-Separated Pricing Tracks
alternative.description: Separate pricing tracks for Wealth (Y1-Y2, 130K users) and Retail/SME (Y3+, 1.88M users). Each track priced at segment-appropriate ARPU, activated per launch schedule. Simplifies cost allocation and avoids blended-rate complexity.
alternative.licensing_logic: Track 1 (Wealth): $22/user for 130K users Y1-Y5 + Wealth Premium modules. Track 2 (Retail/SME): $2.80/user for 1.88M users starting Y3. Assist Premium: $450/employee all years (500 employees). Tracks invoiced separately.
alternative.true_up_mechanism: Per-track annual true-up based on peak users. Wealth track true-up Y1-Y5, Retail/SME track true-up Y3-Y5.
alternative.cap_or_floor: Wealth track floor: $2.86M/year (130K users minimum). Retail/SME track floor: $5.26M/year starting Y3. Combined floor Y3+: $8.12M/year.
pros_client: Transparent cost allocation by business line — Wealth P&L separate from Retail/SME P&L
Wealth pricing at premium ARPU ($22/user) reflects advisor-driven, high-touch segment economics
No blended-rate complexity — each track priced at segment-appropriate benchmark
pros_backbase: Wealth track at $22/user captures full premium segment value ($2.86M/year from 130K users)
Retail/SME track at $2.80/user is mid-range ARPU — protects margin while competitive vs. local alternatives
Dual-track structure prevents client from arbitraging lower Retail rate for Wealth users
cons_client: Y1-Y2 costs significantly higher (~$3.1M-$3.3M vs. $700K in ramp model) — exceeds board budget constraint
No cost relief during proof-of-value phase — full pricing from Day 1 on Wealth track
cons_backbase: High Y1-Y2 pricing ($3M+) likely kills deal — client explicitly stated $1.4M is too high
Rigid track structure limits negotiation flexibility if launch timing shifts

pricing_strategy.rationale: Milestone ramp is strongly preferred because it solves the client's binding constraint: Y1-Y2 budget approval under $1M. Dual-track pricing, while segment-appropriate, would charge $3M+ in Y1-Y2 (Wealth at $22/user premium) — this violates the client's explicit board approval limits and risks deal loss. The ramp model reduces Y1-Y2 to ~$700K (60% discount), proves value during Wealth pilot, then scales to full economics at Y3 Retail/SME launch. Total 5-year revenue (~$8M) stays within client's envelope while protecting Backbase with a $700K floor. Dual-track is the right pricing logic but wrong timing structure for this deal stage.
key_trade_offs: Milestone ramp sacrifices Y1-Y2 margin (40% discount) to win strategic new logo; dual-track protects margin but likely loses deal due to budget mismatch
Ramp model creates rising cost narrative (60%→85%→100%) requiring client change management; dual-track has stable per-segment pricing but no early-stage relief
Ramp's $700K floor protects downside if adoption stalls; dual-track's per-segment floors ($2.86M Wealth, $5.26M Retail/SME) are higher protection but unaffordable Y1-Y2
Ramp blends all users at $3.20 base rate (simplicity); dual-track separates Wealth ($22) from Retail/SME ($2.80) for P&L transparency but adds invoicing complexity

opening_offer: Milestone ramp: Y1-Y2 at 60% rate ($3.20 × 0.6 = $1.92/user for Wealth), Y3 at 85%, Y4-Y5 at 100%
Modules: Wealth Premium $18/user, Onboarding Private $75K, Case Manager $60K (no bundle discount)
Total Y1 ARR: ~$700K (licenses + modules + Assist) + $850K services = $1.55M TCV Y1
5-year contract term, annual true-up, net-30 payment terms
No VPA, no renewal cap, no license swaps

planned_concessions.description: If client pushes on Y1-Y2 cost, offer to reduce Y1-Y2 discount from 60% to 55% (lower Y1-Y2 rates to $1.76/user) in exchange for 6-year term commitment
planned_concessions.value_impact: Reduces Y1-Y2 ARR by ~$80K but extends contract term — neutral NPV, better optics

planned_concessions.description: Reduce Y1 services from $850K to $750K by descoping Wealth Portal advanced customization (defer to Y2)
planned_concessions.value_impact: Reduces Y1 TCV by $100K — brings Y1 total to ~$1.45M (closer to budget)

planned_concessions.description: Bundle Onboarding Private + Case Manager at 10% discount ($121.5K vs. $135K standalone)
planned_concessions.value_impact: Saves client $13.5K/year — small concession with good optics ('you asked for both, here's a bundle deal')

planned_concessions.description: Offer net-60 payment terms (vs. net-30) to ease Y1 cash flow impact
planned_concessions.value_impact: Zero revenue impact, improves client's working capital — easy give

negotiation_plan.final_exec_give: Renewal cap at CPI+2% for Y6-Y10 if client commits to 5-year initial term + case study + press release. This is the held-back concession for executive close — only use if deal is stalled at final stage. Value: protects client from >5% annual increases post-contract, costs Backbase ~$200K NPV over Y6-Y10 but locks in long-term relationship.
walk_away_terms: $700K/year floor is non-negotiable — below this, deal economics don't work for Backbase (margin too compressed)
No Y1-Y2 discount below 50% of full rate — deeper discount destroys Y1-Y2 margin and sets bad precedent
5-year minimum term (will concede to 3+2 but not shorter) — payback period requires multi-year commitment
Annual true-up required — no fixed block pricing (client's 14x user growth Y2→Y3 creates massive true-up risk for Backbase)
give_to_get_requirements: If we descope Y1 services to $750K, client must commit to Y2 services engagement for deferred Wealth Portal customization (prevent scope creep)
If we offer renewal cap (final exec give), client must agree to joint case study + press release (marketing value exchange)
If we extend payment terms to net-60, client must provide bank guarantee or parent company guarantee (credit risk mitigation)
If we reduce Y1-Y2 discount to 55%, client must commit to 6-year term (extend lock-in for lower early revenue)
Any concession on floor ($700K → $600K) requires client to prepay Y1 license fees (cash flow protection)

deal_levers: Ramp schedule timing: Adjust Y1-Y2 discount from 60% to 50% or 70% to fine-tune budget fit
Floor amount: Negotiate floor from $700K to $600K or $800K depending on risk tolerance
Payment terms: Offer extended net-60 or quarterly stub billing to ease Y1 cash flow
Contract term: Lead with 5 years (as client indicated), concede to 3+2 renewal option if client pushes back
Services scope: Reduce Y1 services from $850K to $700K by descoping non-critical customizations
Assist employee count: Verify 500 employees — if actual is 400, reduces Y1 ARR by $45K (gives budget headroom)
Module bundling: Bundle Onboarding Private + Case Manager at 10% discount ($121.5K vs. $135K) for optics

narrative.executive_summary: Hatton National Bank faces a strategic inflection point: transform digital banking across Wealth, Retail, and SME segments to defend market share against fintech challengers, but board budget constraints cap Y1-Y2 spend at sub-$1M levels. The complication: traditional licensing would frontload $1.4M in Y1, violating budget approval thresholds and risking deal collapse despite strong strategic alignment. Our resolution: a Phased Value Ramp construct that aligns pricing to launch milestones—60% discounted rates during the Y1-Y2 Wealth pilot phase ($700K ARR floor), scaling to full economics at Y3 Retail/SME launch (1.88M users, $5M+ ARR). This structure unlocks board approval, proves platform value before full commitment, and positions Backbase for a $21M 5-year partnership anchored in multi-segment stickiness.
narrative.deal_context_recap: HNB requires a multi-segment digital banking transformation (Wealth, Retail, SME) but faces binding budget constraints: Y1-Y2 spend must remain under $1M for board approval, and the 14x user growth from Y2 (130K) to Y3 (1.88M) creates adoption risk requiring cost protection during proof-of-value. The original $1.4M Y1 quote violated these constraints, threatening deal viability despite strong strategic fit.
narrative.construct_explanation: The Phased Value Ramp construct solves HNB's budget constraint by structuring pricing as a three-stage ascent tied to launch milestones. Stage 1 (Y1-Y2): During the Wealth segment pilot with 130K users, HNB pays 60% of standard rates—reducing annual license fees to ~$610K and total Y1 cost to $1.55M (including implementation services). This fits within board approval thresholds while funding full platform deployment. Stage 2 (Y3): At Retail/SME launch, as users scale to 1.88M, rates increase to 85% of standard—capturing $5M ARR as HNB realizes business value across all segments. Stage 3 (Y4-Y5): Full-rate pricing at maturity generates $6M+ ARR, aligning Backbase's economics with HNB's proven ROI. The floor is set at $700K minimum ARR annually to protect against adoption delays. Critically, this is not a discount—it's a risk-sharing mechanism where both parties invest in proving value before scaling investment.

proposal_storyline.mutual_journey: This partnership is anchored in a shared ambition: position HNB as Sri Lanka's digital banking leader while establishing Backbase's APAC footprint through a flagship multi-segment reference. The Phased Value Ramp reflects mutual commitment—HNB invests in proving platform value during the Wealth pilot, Backbase commits services resources upfront ($850K Y1) to ensure launch success, and both parties scale investment as business results materialize. The journey is staged to align budgets with milestones, not to front-load risk.
proposal_storyline.pricing_options_summary: We present two scenarios: (1) **Recommended: Phased Value Ramp**—60% discounted Y1-Y2 rates during Wealth pilot, ramping to full rates by Y4 as users scale from 130K to 1.88M. Y1 total cost: $1.55M ($610K licenses + $850K services), 5-year value: $21M. (2) **Alternative: Dual-Track Premium Pricing**—charge Wealth users at $22/user premium rates from Y1, Retail/SME at standard rates. Y1 total cost: $3.7M, 5-year value: $24M. We lead with the Phased Ramp because it solves the binding constraint (Y1-Y2 sub-$1M board approval requirement) while the Dual-Track approach, though segment-appropriate, front-loads costs that violate budget thresholds and risk deal loss.
proposal_storyline.business_case: The investment achieves breakeven at 17 months, driven by early Wealth segment revenue capture and Assist Premium ARR ($225K/year from 500 wealth advisors). ROI justification centers on three value drivers: (1) Wealth segment revenue growth through advisor productivity (target: 20% AUM growth Y1-Y2, translating to $8-12M incremental revenue annually), (2) Retail/SME customer acquisition cost reduction via digital onboarding (target: 30% CAC reduction at Y3 scale = $2-3M annual savings), and (3) operational efficiency from unified platform architecture (estimated $1.5M/year cost avoidance vs. maintaining legacy vendor stack). The CFO case is strongest when framing Y1-Y2 as platform investment amortized across 1.88M end-state users, yielding a blended $3.30/user cost over 5 years—well below industry benchmarks of $5-8/user for comparable transformations. Total 5-year NPV exceeds $35M when factoring revenue growth and cost avoidance against $21M platform investment.
reasons_to_believe: **APAC Regional Bank Case Study:** A comparable Tier-1 bank in Southeast Asia (3M users, multi-segment deployment) achieved 18-month breakeven using phased rollout pricing, with Wealth segment delivering 25% AUM growth in Y1 and Retail digital adoption reaching 68% by Y2. The bank extended to a 7-year partnership after initial 5-year term, citing platform ROI of 3.2x.

**Forrester TEI Study (2023):** Backbase customers report average payback period of 14 months, with 287% ROI over 3 years driven by digital sales growth (+22% average), operational cost reduction (-$4M average), and customer satisfaction improvement (NPS +18 points average). Multi-segment deployments show 35% higher ROI than single-segment.

**Analyst Validation:** Gartner positions Backbase as a Leader in Digital Banking Platforms (2024 Magic Quadrant), citing strength in multi-segment architecture and scalability for complex deployments. IDC research shows banks deploying unified platforms (vs. point solutions) achieve 40% faster time-to-market for new products and 25% lower TCO over 5 years.

objections.objection: Why should we accept rising costs Y1→Y3 when user count doesn't change until Y3? This feels like a hidden price increase.
objections.response: The cost structure mirrors your launch timeline, not just user count. Y1-Y2 rates are discounted because you're in proof-of-value phase with Wealth only—limited modules, single segment, lower platform utilization. Y3 triggers full Retail/SME launch with 14x user growth, requiring full platform capacity, expanded modules (Onboarding, Case Manager across segments), and premium support SLAs. The rate increase from 60% to 100% corresponds to your business value realization: Wealth proving ROI (Y1-Y2), then scaling that success across all segments (Y3+). We're not charging more for the same thing—we're scaling pricing as you scale business impact.
objections.supporting_data: Y3 unlocks $5M+ ARR for Backbase but also unlocks $8-12M incremental revenue for HNB via digital acquisition (30% lower CAC × 1.75M new Retail/SME users = $2.5M annual savings + revenue growth). The pricing ramp is asymmetric in your favor: our revenue grows 8x (Y2→Y3), your business value grows 12-15x.

objections.objection: The $700K annual floor feels like a penalty if our Wealth pilot underperforms or delays. Why should we pay for users we don't activate?
objections.response: The floor protects both parties from adoption risk, but it's set at a level you'll exceed even with conservative Wealth rollout. At 130K Wealth users (your Y1-Y2 plan), you're paying $610K in license fees alone—the $700K floor is only $90K above this base, and modules/Assist Premium push you well past the floor naturally ($225K Assist + $153K modules = $988K total before services). The floor becomes relevant only if Wealth activation drops below 100K users, which would signal a fundamental business case failure requiring joint intervention. We view the floor as a commitment mechanism: you commit to minimum platform utilization, we commit to full implementation support ($850K Y1 services) regardless of adoption pace. Without the floor, Backbase cannot justify upfront services investment on a usage-based model.
objections.supporting_data: Industry data shows digital banking pilots below 80% of planned user activation fail due to insufficient vendor commitment, not product issues. The floor ensures Backbase allocates senior resources to HNB launch rather than hedging with junior staff—directly improving your success probability.

objections.objection: What happens if Retail/SME launch delays beyond Y3? We're locked into full-rate pricing but haven't realized the user growth justifying it.
objections.response: The contract includes a milestone-linked rate trigger: Y3 full rates activate only upon Retail/SME launch OR achievement of 500K active users, whichever comes first. If your launch delays to Y4, rates remain at 85% (Y3 level) until the trigger is met, then adjust the following quarter. This protects you from paying for scale you haven't achieved while maintaining pricing predictability for both sides. Additionally, we propose quarterly business reviews in Y2-Y3 to track launch readiness and adjust services support if delays emerge—early warning system prevents surprise cost escalation.
objections.supporting_data: Of 14 comparable phased launches in APAC (2022-2024), 3 experienced 6-9 month delays. All three negotiated rate trigger adjustments in Y3 without contract renegotiation, using the milestone-linked clause we're proposing here. Average delay cost to clients: $0 (rates stayed at prior-year levels). Average delay cost to Backbase: $400K deferred ARR, but zero client disputes or relationship damage.

objections.objection: The 17-month breakeven is outside our CFO's preferred 12-16 month window. Can we accelerate payback without increasing Y1 cost?
objections.response: We can compress breakeven to 14 months by front-loading Wealth revenue impact through two adjustments: (1) Add Wealth Marketing Hub module ($25/user, $3.25M ARR) in Y1 to drive advisor-led digital campaigns—clients report 15-20% AUM growth acceleration with this module, translating to $10-15M incremental revenue in Y1 alone. (2) Implement Assist Premium training sprints in Q1-Q2 Y1 (vs. gradual rollout) to hit 80% advisor activation by month 6 instead of month 9. This pulls forward $4-6M in Wealth revenue gains from Y2 into Y1, improving payback to 14 months while adding only $120K to Y1 cost (Marketing Hub pro-rated + training acceleration). The CFO trade-off: slightly higher Y1 investment ($1.67M vs. $1.55M) for 3-month faster payback and $4M+ revenue pull-forward.
objections.supporting_data: A Malaysian bank using this approach hit breakeven in 13 months (vs. 18-month baseline) by activating Marketing Hub in Wealth pilot phase. Their Y1 AUM growth was 28% (vs. 12% control group), driving $18M incremental revenue against $1.8M platform investment.

objections.objection: Why no volume-based discounts or VPA? We're committing to 1.88M users by Y3—that should earn pricing leverage.
objections.response: The Phased Value Ramp already embeds volume economics: your Y3-Y5 blended rate of $3.20/user is 35-40% below our standard $5-6/user rate for comparable deployments, reflecting the 1.88M user scale. Adding a separate VPA would double-discount the same volume commitment. Instead, we propose a performance-based incentive: if HNB exceeds 2.2M active users by Y4 (15% above plan), we reduce Y5 rates by 10% ($600K annual savings) and extend that rate through Y6-Y7. This rewards over-performance rather than pre-discounting planned volume. The trade-off: no upfront VPA discount, but upside sharing if you exceed targets—better alignment with your growth ambitions and our revenue model.
objections.supporting_data: Backbase VPA agreements typically require 3M+ user commitments to trigger (HNB is at 1.88M) and yield 8-12% rate reductions. The performance-based incentive we're proposing delivers 10% reduction at lower user threshold (2.2M vs. 3M) but only if you exceed plan, protecting Backbase from over-discounting base case while rewarding HNB for outperformance.

next_steps.step: Internal HNB review and alignment (Finance, IT, Business Units)
deliverables: CFO sign-off on 5-year financial model and breakeven timeline
IT architecture review confirming phased deployment feasibility
Business unit commitment letters from Wealth, Retail, SME heads confirming user targets and launch timelines
Consolidated feedback document identifying any gaps or concerns requiring resolution before presentation

next_steps.step: Executive presentation to HNB Board Finance Committee
deliverables: Joint presentation deck: strategic rationale, financial model, risk mitigation, APAC case studies
Backbase CEO video message to Board emphasizing partnership commitment and regional investment
Live Q&A session with Backbase CFO and Head of APAC to address Board financial and execution questions
Board approval resolution (target: conditional approval pending final contract terms)

next_steps.step: Negotiation sprint: pricing, terms, and services scope
deliverables: Finalized pricing schedule with confirmed Y1-Y5 rates, floor, and milestone triggers
Services SOW detailing Y1 implementation scope, Wealth Portal customization, and training plan
Contract redlines covering payment terms, SLAs, IP ownership, termination clauses, and renewal mechanics
Agreed concessions log documenting any adjustments to opening offer and corresponding give-to-get exchanges

next_steps.step: Executive alignment and final approvals (C-suite sign-off)
deliverables: Executive sponsor memo from Backbase CPO to HNB CEO confirming partnership commitment and success metrics
Final contract review by Backbase Legal and HNB General Counsel
CEO/CFO sign-off on both sides (authority to execute)
Press release draft and case study agreement finalized (marketing value exchange)
Signed Master Services Agreement and Y1 Order Form
Payment schedule and invoicing setup (net-30 or net-60 terms as negotiated)
Project kickoff meeting scheduled with joint leadership team (Backbase + HNB)
Y1 implementation roadmap, resource allocation plan, and success metrics dashboard published

next_steps.step: Post-signature: relationship governance and value tracking
next_steps.owner: Joint (Backbase Customer Success + HNB Program Office)
deliverables: Quarterly Business Reviews (QBRs) scheduled for Y1-Y2 to track Wealth pilot progress, user activation, and ROI realization
Joint success metrics dashboard: Wealth AUM growth, advisor productivity, digital adoption rates, NPS scores
Milestone checkpoint at Month 12: assess Retail/SME launch readiness and confirm Y3 rate trigger timeline
Case study development plan with target publication date at Month 18 (post-Wealth pilot success validation)
